body:
FINS3646: Toolkit for Finance
Week 1: Lecture

Breno Schmidt and Robert Tumarkin
UNSW




                                    1
 The team

       Less preferred: Email me at r.tumarkin@unsw.edu.au




                                                                        2
 Overview




    Course introduction
    A brief intro to Python & PyCharm
    Coding with AI
    Classes and instances
    Key takeaways




                                        3
 Course introduction
 Course introduction: What you will learn



 This course builds your skills in analysing and working with financial data using Python and AI.




                                                                                                    4
 Course introduction: What you will learn



 This course builds your skills in analysing and working with financial data using Python and AI.

 By the end, you will have experience with:
     Python
         Basic syntax, functions, and modules
         Using Pandas to handle financial data
         Handling “imperfect” data
         Working in PyCharm: a powerful programming environment




                                                                                                    4
 Course introduction: What you will learn



 This course builds your skills in analysing and working with financial data using Python and AI.

 By the end, you will have experience with:
     Python
         Basic syntax, functions, and modules
         Using Pandas to handle financial data
         Handling “imperfect” data
         Working in PyCharm: a powerful programming environment
     Coding with AI:
         Settings for which AI works well and its limits
         Why testing is essential
         Best practices

 By the end, you will have experience with:

                                                                                                    4
 Course introduction: What you will learn



 This course builds your skills in analysing and working with financial data using Python and AI.

 By the end, you will have experience with:
     Python
         Basic syntax, functions, and modules
         Using Pandas to handle financial data
         Handling “imperfect” data
         Working in PyCharm: a powerful programming environment
     Coding with AI:
         Settings for which AI works well and its limits
         Why testing is essential
         Best practices
     Running event studies in Finance


                       We will teach as if you are completely new to programming



                                                                                                    4
 Course introduction: What you will not learn




 This course is not designed to turn you into a Python developer.




                                                                    5
 Course introduction: What you will not learn




 This course is not designed to turn you into a Python developer.

     This is not a Computer Science course
         Our focus is on intuition and application




                                                                    5
 Course introduction: What you will not learn




 This course is not designed to turn you into a Python developer.

     This is not a Computer Science course
         Our focus is on intuition and application
     This is not “principles of programming”
         Most of you do not plan to become Python developers, instead you will use AI as a tool to help
          you write code




                                                                                                           5
 Course introduction: What you will not learn




 This course is not designed to turn you into a Python developer.

      This is not a Computer Science course
          Our focus is on intuition and application
      This is not “principles of programming”
          Most of you do not plan to become Python developers, instead you will use AI as a tool to help
            you write code
          There are many essential topics we will not cover (e.g., creating your own classes, design patterns)

 Our goal is to give you just enough Python to use AI effectively and solve real problems in financial research
                                                and analysis

                                                                                8
 Pre-recorded Lessons


   1. Introduction to Python
         Introducing Python
         Classes, instances, and objects – Intuition
         Python basics
   2. Building blocks
         Constructors, literals, basic types
         Identifiers and names
         Assignment statements
         Rules and conventions
   3. Data structures
         Data structures: Lists, tuples, sets, and dictionaries
         Data structures and references
   4. Control flow
         Boolean logic and conditional execution
         Control flow – Loops (Parts I and II)
         Comprehensions
         Control flow – Functions
   5. Modules
         Modules and packages, docstrings
         IO and paths
   6. Namespaces and scope
   7. Event studies in Finance
   8. Pandas (Parts I and II)

      This is not our weekly schedule. Please refer to ed to find out what will be discussed each week.

                                                                                                          9
 A brief introduction to Python
 A simple task




 Think about how you comprehend and process the simple task below.

                         Task: What is the value of x when x   1   2?




                                                                         10
 A simple task




 Think about how you comprehend and process the simple task below.

                           Task: What is the value of x when x   1   2?


     Your brain can answer this instantly.
     A computer can also calculate 1      2,
        But it only understands machine code (sequences of 0s and 1s).




                                                                           10
 A simple task




 Think about how you comprehend and process the simple task below.

                           Task: What is the value of x when x   1   2?


     Your brain can answer this instantly.
     A computer can also calculate 1      2,
        But it only understands machine code (sequences of 0s and 1s).
     Since we don’t “speak” machine code, we need a translator.
        A programming language allows us to communicate with that translator.




                                                                                 10
 A simple task




 Think about how you comprehend and process the simple task below.

                           Task: What is the value of x when x   1   2?


     Your brain can answer this instantly.
     A computer can also calculate 1      2,
        But it only understands machine code (sequences of 0s and 1s).
     Since we don’t “speak” machine code, we need a translator.
        A programming language allows us to communicate with that translator.




                                                                                 10
 Using Python as a translator




 Intuition:




                               11
 Using Python as a translator




 For our purposes, we can think of Python as both:
     A programming language that lets us write instructions clearly.
     A translator program, called the Python interpreter, that runs those instructions.




                                                                                          12
 Using Python as a translator




 For our purposes, we can think of Python as both:
     A programming language that lets us write instructions clearly.
     A translator program, called the Python interpreter, that runs those instructions.




                                                                                          12
 Using Python as a translator




 For our purposes, we can think of Python as both:
     A programming language that lets us write instructions clearly.
     A translator program, called the Python interpreter, that runs those instructions.




    Note: In reality this process is more complex. The Python interpreter first compiles your code into
     “bytecode” (not machine code). Then the Python Virtual Machine translates that bytecode into
                                          machine-level operations.




                                                                                                          12
 Using Python as a translator




 In this course, we will focus on how we communicate with Python—not how Python communicates with the
 CPU.




                                                                                                        13
 Using PyCharm to communicate with Python




 PyCharm puts this workflow together, so you can easily send instructions to the Python interpreter.




                                                                                                       14
 Coding with AI
 Coding with AI




 Below is a list of topics we discussed in class

     We asked ChatGPT the following question:
         If I want to use AI to help me code in Python, do I really need to learn Python at all?




                                                                                                    15
 Coding with AI




 Below is a list of topics we discussed in class

     We asked ChatGPT the following question:
         If I want to use AI to help me code in Python, do I really need to learn Python at all?
      Using AI: Writing Emails vs. Coding
         Ask ChatGPT: Can you help me write an email asking for details about a job position at a bank
           in Hong Kong?
         I argued that before sending the email we must:
             1. Understand the email
             2. “Test” the email (by reading it) to ensure it communicates what we want
         In the same way, when using AI to write code we must:
             • Understand the basics of the programming language
             • Test the code to confirm it behaves as intended




                                                                                                          15
 Using AI: Writing Emails vs. Coding




                                      16
 Using AI: Writing Emails vs. Coding




                                      17
 Using AI: Writing Emails vs. Coding




                                      17
 Classes and instances - Intuition
 Classes and instances - Intuition




          Python is an object-oriented language. It is important that you start you Fintech
                          journey by understanding this concept intuitively.




                                                                                              18
 Intuition: Instances and classes
 Intuition: Instances and classes




 Consider the iPhones in the room – these are physical objects manufactured by a factory.




                                                                                            19
 Intuition: Instances and classes




 Consider the iPhones in the room – these are physical objects manufactured by a factory.

                                                                             Class (Factory)

                                                                                  iPhone




                                                                                               19
 Intuition: Instances and classes




 Consider the iPhones in the room – these are physical objects manufactured by a factory.

                                                                               Class (Factory)

                                                                                    iPhone




            We will say these factories produce a specific class of physical objects,
                                    and represent them in yellow boxes.




                                                                                                 19
 Intuition: Instances and classes




 Our actual, physical phones are objects – distinct instances of an iPhone.

                                                                              Class (Factory)

                                                                                  iPhone




                                                                                                20
 Intuition: Instances and classes




 Our actual, physical phones are objects – distinct instances of an iPhone.

            Instance (Object)                                                 Class (Factory)

        lecturers_device :   iPhone                                               iPhone




        students_device :    iPhone




                                                                                                20
 Intuition: Instances and classes




 Our actual, physical phones are objects – distinct instances of an iPhone.

            Instance (Object)                                                 Class (Factory)

        lecturers_device :   iPhone                                                   iPhone




        students_device :    iPhone




             We will represent these physical instances (objects) using gray boxes,
                                noting the class (factory) that created them.




                                                                                                20
 Intuition: Instances and classes




                           Instance (Object) Notation: <identifier> : <class>


           Instance (Object)                                             Class (Factory)

       lecturers_device :   iPhone                                              iPhone




       students_device :    iPhone




     <identifier> – a unique object name
     <class> – the factory that produced the object




                                                                                           21
 Intuition: Instances and classes




 Let’s pause and summarise what we’ve learned:

      Instances are specific physical objects.

      Classes are factories that produce objects.

      Every instance MUST belong to a class.




                                                    22
 Intuition: Attributes
 Intuition: Attributes




 Physical objects produced by a factory share common intrinsic characteristics (attributes).

           Instances (Object)                                                 Class (Factory)

        lecturers_device:   iPhone                                                 iPhone

                                                                           .owner : Person
                                                                           .appstore_id: UserId



         students_device:   iPhone




                Similarly, instances that belong to the same class share common attributes.




                                                                                                  23
 Intuition: Attributes




 Physical objects produced by a factory share common intrinsic characteristics (attributes).

           Instances (Object)                                                  Class (Factory)

        lecturers_device:   iPhone                                                  iPhone

                                                                            .owner : Person
                                                                            .appstore_id: UserId



         students_device:   iPhone




                        We will write attributes using a leading dot (decimal point)




                                                                                                   24
 Intuition: Attributes




 While we take note of the attributes produced in the factory, each manufactured object must have the
 attribute.

           Instances (Object)                                                  Class (Factory)

        lecturers_device:   iPhone                                                    iPhone

       .owner: Person = Lecturer                                            .owner : Person
       .appstore_id: UserId = abc                                           .appstore_id: UserId




         students_device:   iPhone

       .owner: Person = Student
       .appstore_id: UserId = def




                            Every attribute has a value specific to every instance.




                                                                                                        25
 Intuition: Attributes


                           Attribute Notation: .<attribute>: <type> = <value>


          Instances (Object)                                             Class (Factory)

       lecturers_device:    iPhone                                              iPhone

      .owner: Person = Lecturer                                       .owner : Person
      .appstore_id: UserId = abc                                      .appstore_id: UserId




        students_device:    iPhone

      .owner: Person = Student
      .appstore_id: UserId = def



     <attribute> – the name of the attribute
     <type> – the kind of thing the attribute describes
     <value> – the value of the attribute for that specific instance

  <type> is the class of the attribute. We use this term to distinguish the type of information
                stored in the object’s attribute from the class of the object itself.

                                                                                                  26
 Intuition: Attributes




 Let’s pause and summarise what we’ve learned:

      Instances and classes:
         Instances are specific physical objects.
         Classes are conceptual categories.
         Every instance MUST belong to a class.
     Attributes:
         Attributes are intrinsic characteristics shared by all instances of a class.
         The attributes define what it is to be a class.




                                                                                         27
 Intuition: Methods
 Intuition: Methods




 Physical objects produced by a factory also share common actions that you can perform with them.


 Actions can require just a single object. . .
      students_phone.check_time() : Time

 . . . or may multiple objects
      students_phone.call(friends_phone) : Conversation

      students_phone.purchase_coffee(cafe) : Flat_White




                                                                                                    28
 Intuition: Methods




 Physical objects produced by a factory also share common actions that you can perform with them.


 Actions can require just a single object. . .
      students_phone.check_time() : Time

 . . . or may multiple objects
      students_phone.call(friends_phone) : Conversation

      students_phone.purchase_coffee(cafe) : Flat_White



              We will use the term method to refer to actions one can perform with objects.




                                                                                                    28
 Intuition: Methods




 Physical objects produced by a factory also share common actions that you can perform with them.


 Methods need not accomplish anything. . .
      students_phone.admire_phone() : None
      students_phone.tiktok() : None




                                                                                                    29
 Intuition: Methods




 Physical objects produced by a factory also share common actions that you can perform with them.


 Methods need not accomplish anything. . .
      students_phone.admire_phone() : None
      students_phone.tiktok() : None



            We signify that the the method didn’t produce a result object with the word None.




                                                                                                    29
 Intuition: Methods




 Physical objects produced by a factory also share common actions that you can perform with them.

           Instances (Object)                                              Class (Factory)

        lecturers_device:   iPhone                                              iPhone

   .owner: Person = Lecturer                                      .owner : Person
   .appstore_id: UserId = abc                                     .appstore_id: UserId

   .call(Phone): Conversation                                     .call(Phone): Conversation
   .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite                              .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite
   .tiktok(): None                                                .tiktok(): None



         students_device:   iPhone

   .owner: Person = Student
   .appstore_id: UserId = def

   .call(Phone): Conversation
   .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite
   .tiktok(): None




                                                                                                      30
 Intuition: Methods




                        Method Notation: .<method>(parameters) : <type>


     <method> – the name of the action
     <parameters> – other objects required to perform the method
         Any other objects required by the action are listed in parentheses (other1, other2...)
         Empty parentheses () are used if the action doesn’t require other objects.
     <type> – the type of object that results from performing the action




                                                                                                   31
 Intuition: Methods




                         Method Notation: .<method>(parameters) : <type>


      <method> – the name of the action
      <parameters> – other objects required to perform the method
          Any other objects required by the action are listed in parentheses (other1, other2...)
          Empty parentheses () are used if the action doesn’t require other objects.
      <type> – the type of object that results from performing the action



 <type> is the class of the object that results from the action. We use this term to distinguish it
                                  from the class of the object itself.




                                                                                                      31
 Intuition: Methods




                         Method Notation: .<method>(parameters) : <type>


      <method> – the name of the action
      <parameters> – other objects required to perform the method
          Any other objects required by the action are listed in parentheses (other1, other2...)
          Empty parentheses () are used if the action doesn’t require other objects.
      <type> – the type of object that results from performing the action



 <type> is the class of the object that results from the action. We use this term to distinguish it
                                  from the class of the object itself.


         Methods may do much more than provide a result, creating some complexity.
                              We will cover this in later lectures.




                                                                                                      31
 Intuition: Methods




 Just as we need an object to perform an action, we need an instance to a call (execute) a method:

           Instances (Object)                                          Calling (Executing) Methods

        lecturers_device:   iPhone                         Examples:

       .owner: Person = Lecturer                                 lecturers_device.check_time()
       .appstore_id: UserId = abc                                students_device.check_time()
       .check_time()



         students_device:   iPhone

       .owner: Person = Student
       .appstore_id: UserId = def

       .check_time()



               We call methods using an instance name followed by a dot and parentheses.




                                                                                                     32
 Intuition: Methods




 Let’s pause and summarise what we’ve learned:

      Instances and classes:
         Instances are specific physical objects.
         Classes are conceptual categories.
         Every instance MUST belong to a class.
     Attributes:
         Attributes are intrinsic characteristics shared by all instances of a class.
         The attributes define what it is to be a class.
      Methods:
         Methods are actions that one can perform with an object. An instance is needed to call (execute)
          a method.
         Methods are shared by all instances of a class.



                                                                                                             33
 Intuition: Class hierarchy
 Intuition: Class hierarchy


 Despite Apple’s best marketing effort, someone in this class may use a Galaxy phone:

           Instances (Object)                                                Class (Factory)

        lecturers_device:   iPhone                                                iPhone

    .owner: Person = Lecturer                                      .owner : Person
    .appstore_id: UserId = abc                                     .appstore_id: UserId

    .call(Phone): Conversation                                     .call(Phone): Conversation
    .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite                              .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite
    .tiktok(): None                                                .tiktok(): None
    .facetime(iPhone): VideoCall                                   .facetime(iPhone): VideoCall


         students_device:   Galaxy                                                Galaxy

    .owner: Person = Student                                       .owner: Person
    .galaxy_store_id: GalaxyId = def                               .galaxy_store_id:    GalaxyId

    .call(Phone): Conversation                                     .call(Phone): Conversation
    .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite                              .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite
    .tiktok(): None                                                .tiktok(): None
    .powershare(EarBuds): None                                     .powershare(EarBuds): None



  iPhones and Galaxy smart phones share some attributes and methods, but also have some that are brand
                                            (class) specific.


                                                                                                         34
 Intuition: Class hierarchy


 Treating every object as having a distinct class is inefficient. People often understand things by organising
 them into a conceptual hierarchy.

                                              Class (Factory)

                                                  SmartPhone




                        iPhone                                                   Galaxy

         .owner : Person                                          .owner: Person
         .appstore_id: UserId                                     .galaxy_store_id:   GalaxyId

         .call(Phone): Conversation                               .call(Phone): Conversation
         .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite                        .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite
         .tiktok(): None                                          .tiktok(): None
         .facetime(iPhone): VideoCall                             .powershare(EarBuds): None



                 iPhones and Galaxy phones are both types of smart phones.
                                  So, we can create a SmartPhone class.

                                                                                                                 35
 Intuition: Class hierarchy


 We can move attributes and methods to the SmartPhone class and link our iPhone and Galaxy classes to it.

                                             Class (Factory)

                                                SmartPhone

                                   .owner:   Person

                                   .call(Phone): Conversation
                                   .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite
                                   .tiktok(): None




                       iPhone                                                Galaxy

         .owner : Person                                       .owner: Person
         .appstore_id: UserId                                  .galaxy_store_id:   GalaxyId

         .call(Phone): Conversation                            .call(Phone): Conversation
         .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite                     .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite
         .tiktok(): None                                       .tiktok(): None
         .facetime(iPhone): VideoCall                          .powershare(EarBuds): None




                                                                                                            36
 Intuition: Class hierarchy


 We can move attributes and methods to the SmartPhone class and link our iPhone and Galaxy classes to it.

                                                Class (Factory)

                                                   SmartPhone

                                      .owner:   Person

                                      .call(Phone): Conversation
                                      .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite
                                      .tiktok(): None




                        iPhone                                                   Galaxy

         .owner : Person                                          .owner: Person
         .appstore_id: UserId                                     .galaxy_store_id:   GalaxyId

         .call(Phone): Conversation                               .call(Phone): Conversation
         .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite                        .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite
         .tiktok(): None                                          .tiktok(): None
         .facetime(iPhone): VideoCall                             .powershare(EarBuds): None



 We use an arrow to link a child class with specific functionality to a parent class with general functionality.

                                                                                                                   36
 Intuition: Class hierarchy




 Each attribute and method appears once our hierarchy is cleaned.

                                               Class (Factory)

                                                  SmartPhone

                                     .owner:   Person

                                     .call(Phone): Conversation
                                     .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite
                                     .tiktok(): None




                       iPhone                                                 Galaxy

            .appstore_id:   UserId                                  .galaxy_store_id:   GalaxyId

            .facetime(iPhone):   VideoCall                          .powershare(EarBuds):   None




                                                                                                   37
 Intuition: Class hierarchy




 Each attribute and method appears once our hierarchy is cleaned.

                                               Class (Factory)

                                                  SmartPhone

                                     .owner:   Person

                                     .call(Phone): Conversation
                                     .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite
                                     .tiktok(): None




                       iPhone                                                 Galaxy

            .appstore_id:   UserId                                  .galaxy_store_id:   GalaxyId

            .facetime(iPhone):   VideoCall                          .powershare(EarBuds):   None




                                                                                                   38
 Intuition: Class hierarchy


 The hierarchy affects of class structure. Our instance get attributes and methods from their class and any
 parent class above.

                                                      Class (Factory)
                                                            SmartPhone

                                               .owner:   Person

                                               .call(Phone): Conversation
                                               .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite
                                               .tiktok(): None




                                iPhone                                                 Galaxy

                    .appstore_id:   UserId                                  .galaxy_store_id:    GalaxyId

                    .facetime(iPhone):    VideoCall                         .powershare(EarBuds):    None




                                                   Instances (Object)
                      lecturers_device:   iPhone                              students_device:   Galaxy

                  .owner: Person = Lecturer                              .owner: Person = Student
                  .appstore_id: UserId = abc                             .galaxy_store_id: GalaxyId = def

                  .call(Phone): Conversation                             .call(Phone): Conversation
                  .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite                      .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite
                  .tiktok(): None                                        .tiktok(): None
                  .facetime(iPhone): VideoCall                           .powershare(EarBuds): None


                                                                                                              39
 Intuition: Class hierarchy




 Let’s pause and summarise what we’ve learned:

      Instances and classes:
         Instances are specific physical objects.
         Classes are conceptual categories.
         Every instance MUST belong to a class.
         Classes may form a hierarchy, where a child class is a specific form of a parent class.
     Attributes:
         Attributes are intrinsic characteristics shared by all instances of a class.
         The attributes define what it is to be a class.
         Instances get the attributes of their class and all their parent classes.
      Methods:
         Methods are actions that one can perform with an object. An instance is needed to call (execute)
          a method.
         Methods are shared by all instances of a class.
         Instances get the methods of their class and all their parent classes.


                                                                                                             40
 Looking forward: Objects in Python
 Objects in Python


 Objects in Python parallel our example with Smart Phones:

                                                     Class (Factory)
                                                          SmartPhone

                                             .owner:   Person

                                             .call(Phone): Conversation
                                             .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite
                                             .tiktok(): None




                               iPhone                                                Galaxy

                    .appstore_id:   UserId                                .galaxy_store_id:    GalaxyId

                    .facetime(iPhone):   VideoCall                        .powershare(EarBuds):    None




                                                  Instances (Object)
                      students_device:   Galaxy                            lecturers_device:   iPhone

                 .owner: Person = Student                              .owner: Person = Lecturer
                 .galaxy_store_id: GalaxyId = def                      .appstore_id: UserId = abc

                 .call(Phone): Conversation                            .call(Phone): Conversation
                 .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite                     .purchase_coffee(Cafe): FlatWhite
                 .tiktok(): None                                       .tiktok(): None
                 .powershare(EarBuds): None                            .facetime(iPhone): VideoCall



                                                                                                           41
 Objects in Python




 Classes serve as factories. They define shared attributes and methods, and they are used to create instances:

      Attributes are intrinsic characteristics shared by all instances of a class.
      Methods are actions that one can perform with an object shared by all instances of a class.
      Classes may form a hierarchy, where a child class is a specific form of a parent class.
      We use the class as a factory to make object instances.

 Instances are specific objects stored in the computer’s memory.

      Every instance MUST belong to a class.
      Instances get the attributes of their class and all their parent classes.
      Instances get the methods of their class and all their parent classes.




                                                                                                                 42
 UML Diagrams



 The figures we have been using are UML (Unified Modelling Language) diagrams:

                                                      Class (Factory)
                                                            <class>

                                                .<attribute>:   <type>

                                                .<method>(<parameters>):   <type>




                             <class_a>                                                   <class_b>

                  .<attribute_a>:   <type>                                 .<attribute_b>:      <type>

                  .method_a(<parameters>):   <type>                        .method_b(<parameters>):      <type>




                                                  Instances (Object)
                        object_2:   <class_b>                                       object_1:   <class_a>

                 .<attribute>: <type> = <value>                            .<attribute>: <type> = <value>
                 .<attribute_b>: <type> = <value>                          .<attribute_a>: <type> = <value>

                 .<method>(<parameters>): <type>                           .<method>(<parameters>): <type>
                 .<method_b>(<parameters>): <type>                         .<method_a>(<parameters>): <type>

 Key takeaways
 Key takeaways



    Python is an object-oriented language
       An object is an instance of a class
       Classes define methods and attributes shared by all their instances
       Classes have a hierarchy
          • A child class inherits all the functionality of its parent class

    Python lets us instruct a computer to perform tasks
       The Python interpreter translates our code into machine-level operations
